_about: Canonical English source for all 35 protocols. generate-translations.mjs reads this and produces translations.js entries for Marathi / Tamil / Telugu / Bengali via Google Cloud Translation. Keep this file in sync if the protocol text in index.html changes.

unconscious-breathing.title: Unconscious but breathing
steps: Tap the shoulder and shout. Check for a response.
Open the airway: gently tilt the head back and lift the chin.
Watch the chest rise for ten seconds to confirm breathing.
Roll the victim onto the left side — recovery position. Top knee bent forward.
Keep the airway open. Stay with the victim. Check breathing every minute.
dontDo: Do NOT give water or food.
Do NOT leave the victim alone.
Do NOT move the neck if a spine injury is suspected.

cpr.title: CPR — not breathing
steps: Lay the victim flat on the ground, face up, on a hard surface.
Place the heel of one palm on the centre of the chest. Put your other hand on top.
Push hard and fast — 5 to 6 cm deep, about 2 per second. Count to thirty.
If trained, give two rescue breaths. If not, keep pressing.
Repeat thirty pushes and two breaths until the ambulance arrives or the victim moves.
dontDo: Do NOT stop to check the pulse repeatedly.
Do NOT lift your hands fully between pushes.
Do NOT give up before ten minutes or until the ambulance takes over.

severe-bleeding.title: Severe bleeding
steps: Wear gloves if available, or use any cloth between your hand and the blood.
Press hard directly on the wound with the cleanest cloth you can find.
Keep pressing. Do not lift the cloth to check. Add more cloth on top if it soaks through.
Raise the bleeding limb above the heart, only if no fracture is suspected.
Tie a firm bandage around the cloth to keep the pressure on.
Watch for shock — pale skin, sweating, fast shallow breathing.
dontDo: Do NOT remove an object embedded in the wound.
Do NOT use a tourniquet unless the limb is almost severed.
Do NOT give water if surgery may be needed.

spinal-injury.title: Suspected spinal injury
steps: Do NOT move the victim. Tell them to stay still.
Kneel at the head. Hold the head still with both hands, ears between palms.
Tell the victim to breathe slowly. Reassure calmly.
Keep head, neck and back in one straight line until the ambulance arrives.
If you MUST move them for safety, log-roll with helpers — keep the spine straight.
dontDo: Do NOT lift the head or bend the neck.
Do NOT sit them up or stand them up.
Do NOT remove a helmet unless you are trained.

multiple-casualties.title: Multiple casualties
steps: Shout: anyone who can walk, come towards me. Walkers are GREEN.
For each remaining victim, check breathing. Not breathing after a head-tilt is BLACK.
Fast breathing (more than 30 per minute) or heavy bleeding is RED.
Calm breathing and conscious but cannot walk is YELLOW.
Treat RED first: stop bleeding, open airways. Tell bystanders to help GREEN victims.
Call 108. Report the count of RED, YELLOW, GREEN victims and the road landmark.
dontDo: Do NOT try to save BLACK before RED.
Do NOT move spinal cases unless there is fire or traffic.
Do NOT work alone if helpers are available.

burns.title: Burns
steps: Move the victim away from the heat source — only if it is safe for you.
Pour clean cool (not ice-cold) water on the burn for at least twenty minutes.
Remove rings, watches, tight clothing near the burn before swelling begins.
Cover the burn loosely with clean cling film, plastic wrap or a non-stick cloth.
Keep the rest of the body warm. Do not break blisters.
dontDo: Do NOT apply ice, butter, toothpaste, oil or ointment.
Do NOT peel off cloth stuck to the burn.
Do NOT pop blisters.

fractures.title: Fractures
steps: Do not try to straighten the broken limb.
Support the limb in the exact position you found it.
Pad around the injury with rolled cloth or a folded jacket.
Splint to a rigid object — stick, rolled magazine — tying above and below the break.
Check the fingers or toes stay warm and pink. Loosen if they turn pale or cold.
dontDo: Do NOT push a protruding bone back in.
Do NOT give food or water — surgery may be needed.
Do NOT realign the limb.

shock.title: Shock
steps: Lay the victim flat on the back.
Raise the legs about thirty cm on a bag or jacket — unless leg or spine is injured.
Loosen tight clothing at neck, chest and waist.
Cover with a jacket or blanket to keep the body warm.
Talk to them calmly. Check breathing every minute.
dontDo: Do NOT give water, food, or alcohol.
Do NOT let them stand or walk.
Do NOT leave them alone.

child-victim.title: Child or infant victim
steps: Call 108 immediately and say it is a child. Children worsen quickly.
Open the airway gently — over-tilting can block a small airway.
For CPR on a child, use the heel of one hand. Push about five cm deep.
For an infant under one year, use two fingers. Push about four cm deep.
Rescue breaths for an infant: cover the mouth AND nose with your mouth.
Continue thirty pushes and two breaths until help arrives.
dontDo: Do NOT shake the child.
Do NOT use adult compression force.
Do NOT give water.

pregnant-victim.title: Pregnant victim
steps: Tilt the victim onto her LEFT side, about thirty degrees, using a folded jacket under the right hip.
This relieves pressure on the big vein and helps blood return to the heart.
If she stops breathing, start CPR. Push on the breastbone as normal.
If she must stay on her back, manually push the belly to the left side.
Watch for bleeding from below or sudden belly hardness.
Tell 108 that this is a pregnant patient so they send the right team.
dontDo: Do NOT lay her flat on her back for long.
Do NOT press on the belly.
Do NOT give water.

head-injury.title: Head injury / concussion
steps: Keep the victim still and lying down. Treat as a spine injury until proven safe.
Hold the head in line with the body. Do not turn the neck.
Apply gentle pressure on any scalp bleeding with a clean cloth — do not press over a depressed skull.
Watch for warning signs: vomiting, one pupil bigger, slurred speech, confusion, fluid from ear or nose.
If they vomit, log-roll to the side with the spine straight to protect the airway.
Keep talking to them. Note time of injury and any change in consciousness.
dontDo: Do NOT let them sleep without monitoring.
Do NOT give aspirin or pain killers — may worsen bleeding.
Do NOT remove a helmet unless trained.

crush-injury.title: Crush injury (trapped limb)
steps: Call 108 immediately. Say a limb is trapped under a vehicle or heavy object.
Do NOT lift the weight off if it has been trapped more than 15 minutes — wait for medics.
Control any bleeding above the crushed area with firm pressure on a clean cloth.
Keep the victim warm with a jacket or blanket. Shock is very likely.
Talk to them. Do NOT give water — surgery is likely needed.
If trapped less than 15 minutes AND it is safe, lift the weight off, control bleeding, and watch breathing.
dontDo: Do NOT release the weight without medics if trapped >15 min — toxins can stop the heart.
Do NOT give pain killers.
Do NOT leave the victim alone.

choking.title: Choking — airway blocked
steps: Ask: 'Are you choking?'. If they can cough or speak, encourage them to keep coughing.
If they cannot cough or speak, stand behind. Lean them forward.
Give five firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
If still blocked: wrap arms around waist, fist above the navel, five inward and upward thrusts.
Repeat five back blows and five thrusts until the object comes out.
If they become unconscious, lay them down and start CPR immediately.
dontDo: Do NOT slap the back if they are still coughing well.
Do NOT give water.
Do NOT use abdominal thrusts on a pregnant victim — use chest thrusts.

amputation.title: Severed finger or limb
steps: Call 108 and stay with the victim. Lay them down and keep them calm.
Apply very firm direct pressure on the stump with the cleanest cloth you have.
Raise the injured limb above the heart if possible. Keep pressing.
Wrap the severed part in a clean cloth. Place in a clean plastic bag. Seal.
Place that bag in a second bag with ice and water — never let ice touch the tissue.
Send the cooled part with the patient to the hospital, labelled with the time.
dontDo: Do NOT wash the severed part with soap or antiseptic.
Do NOT put the part directly on ice.
Do NOT use a tourniquet unless bleeding cannot be stopped any other way.

eye-injury.title: Eye injury / object in eye
steps: Tell the victim to keep both eyes still — the eyes move together, so moving one moves the other.
If an object is stuck in the eye, do NOT remove it.
Build a ring of rolled cloth around the object to stop pressure on it. Tape it in place.
Cover both eyes with a soft pad — covering only one still lets the injured eye move.
For chemical splash without an embedded object, rinse with clean water for 15 minutes.
Keep the head still. Do not let the victim rub the eye.
dontDo: Do NOT remove an embedded object.
Do NOT press on the eyeball.
Do NOT rinse if a sharp object is stuck.

seizure.title: Seizure / fit
steps: Move sharp objects, traffic, and furniture away from the victim.
Cushion the head with a folded jacket or bag.
Loosen any tight clothing at the neck.
Time the seizure. Note when it started.
When the shaking stops, gently roll them onto their side — recovery position.
Call 108 if the seizure lasts over 5 minutes, repeats, or it is the first one ever.
dontDo: Do NOT put anything in the mouth — they cannot swallow their tongue.
Do NOT hold them down.
Do NOT give water until fully awake.

heart-attack.title: Heart attack / chest pain
steps: Sit the victim down with back and head supported, knees bent. Loosen tight clothing.
Call 108 immediately. Say 'suspected heart attack' so they send the right team.
Ask about allergies. If adult, no allergy, no bleeding — give one regular aspirin (300 mg) to chew slowly, if available.
Keep them still and reassure them calmly. Do not let them walk.
Watch breathing every minute. Be ready to start CPR if they stop responding.
dontDo: Do NOT have them walk or drive.
Do NOT give aspirin if they are bleeding or allergic.
Do NOT give nitroglycerin unless their own doctor has prescribed it.

stroke.title: Stroke — F.A.S.T.
steps: F — Face: ask them to smile. Does one side droop?
A — Arms: ask them to raise both arms. Does one drift down?
S — Speech: ask them to repeat a simple sentence. Is it slurred or wrong?
T — Time: note the exact time symptoms began. This decides treatment.
Call 108. Say 'suspected stroke' and the time symptoms started.
Lay them down with the head and shoulders slightly raised. Keep them still and warm.
dontDo: Do NOT give food or water — they may not be able to swallow.
Do NOT give aspirin — strokes can be bleeding type.
Do NOT delay the call.

smoke-inhalation.title: Smoke inhalation / vehicle fire
steps: Get the victim into fresh air at once — only if you can do it without entering the smoke yourself.
Loosen clothing at the neck and chest. Sit them up, slightly leaning forward.
Look around the mouth and nose for soot or singed hair — that warns of burned airway.
Listen for hoarse voice, noisy breathing, or coughing black phlegm — all are emergencies.
Call 108 and say 'smoke inhalation'. The airway can swell shut over the next hour.
Monitor breathing every minute. Be ready to start CPR if they stop responding.
dontDo: Do NOT re-enter the smoke yourself.
Do NOT let them lie flat if they are coughing or struggling to breathe.
Do NOT give water if they are confused or drowsy.

electric-shock.title: Electric shock / downed wire
steps: Do NOT touch the victim while they are still in contact with the source.
Switch off the power at the main switch or pull the plug.
If you cannot turn it off, use DRY wood, plastic, or rubber to push the source away.
Check breathing. If not breathing, start CPR immediately.
Look for entry and exit burns — usually hand and foot. Cover with clean cloth.
Treat for shock: keep warm, lay flat, monitor. Call 108 — internal injury is common.
dontDo: Do NOT touch a live victim with bare hands.
Do NOT use wet or metal objects to push wires.
Do NOT skip the hospital even if they feel fine — heart rhythm can change later.

diabetic-emergency.title: Diabetic emergency (low sugar)
steps: Ask if they are diabetic. Look for sweating, shaking, confusion, slurred speech, hunger.
If conscious, give 15 grams of fast sugar — fruit juice, regular soft drink, sugar in water, or glucose biscuit.
Help them sit down. Stay with them. Keep talking calmly.
Recheck after 10 minutes. If still confused, give another 15 grams of sugar.
If they pass out, do NOT give anything by mouth. Place in recovery position. Call 108.
dontDo: Do NOT force food or drink if unconscious.
Do NOT give diet or sugar-free drinks.
Do NOT leave them alone.

asthma-attack.title: Asthma attack
steps: Help them sit up straight, leaning slightly forward. Loosen tight clothing.
Ask for their inhaler. Help them shake it and breathe in two puffs.
Wait one minute. If no improvement, give two more puffs. Repeat up to 10 puffs.
Talk calmly. Tell them slow breaths in through the nose, out through pursed lips.
Call 108 if no inhaler is available, if attack worsens, lips turn blue, or no relief after 10 puffs.
dontDo: Do NOT make them lie flat.
Do NOT give water during a severe attack.
Do NOT panic — your calm helps their breathing.

anaphylaxis.title: Severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis)
steps: Look for swollen lips/tongue/face, hives, wheezing, throat tightness, dizziness.
Call 108 right away. Say 'severe allergic reaction'.
If they have an EpiPen, help them inject it into the OUTER thigh — even through clothes.
Lay them flat. Raise the legs about 30 cm. If breathing is hard, let them sit instead.
Remove the trigger if visible — bee sting (scrape, do not pinch), food still in mouth.
If a second EpiPen is available and symptoms return in 5–15 min, use it.
dontDo: Do NOT have them stand or walk.
Do NOT delay the EpiPen — adrenaline saves lives.
Do NOT give food or water.

fainting.title: Fainting / collapse
steps: Lay the victim flat on their back on a safe surface.
Raise the legs about 30 cm using a bag or jacket.
Loosen tight clothing at the neck, chest, and waist. Make sure fresh air can reach them.
Check breathing. If awake, keep them lying down for several minutes.
When they sit up, do it slowly. If they faint again, call 108.
dontDo: Do NOT stand them up quickly.
Do NOT splash water on their face.
Do NOT give water until they are fully awake.

heatstroke.title: Heatstroke
steps: Move the victim to shade or a cool indoor place immediately.
Remove extra clothing. Loosen belts.
Cool aggressively: pour cool water on body, fan them, wet cloths to neck, armpits, and groin.
If awake and clear, sip cool water — slow, small sips.
Call 108. Heatstroke can damage the brain and kidneys quickly.
Keep cooling until temperature feels normal or help arrives.
dontDo: Do NOT give paracetamol or aspirin — they do not help heatstroke.
Do NOT give alcohol or sugary drinks.
Do NOT use ice baths on elderly or weak victims.

hypothermia.title: Hypothermia (severe cold)
steps: Move the victim to a warm sheltered area. Handle very gently — a cold heart can stop with rough handling.
Carefully remove wet clothes. Pat skin dry without rubbing.
Wrap them in dry blankets including the head. Use body heat — skin to skin — if needed.
If fully awake and able to swallow, give warm (not hot) sweet drinks.
Call 108. Keep monitoring breathing.
dontDo: Do NOT rub the skin or limbs.
Do NOT give alcohol or coffee.
Do NOT use direct heat (fire, hot water bath, heating pad).

nosebleed.title: Nosebleed
steps: Sit the victim up. Lean head SLIGHTLY FORWARD — do not tilt back.
Pinch the SOFT part of the nose (just below the bone) firmly with thumb and finger.
Hold the pinch for a full 10 minutes without releasing to check. Breathe through the mouth.
Spit out any blood in the mouth. Place a cold cloth on the bridge of the nose.
If still bleeding after 10 minutes, pinch again for another 10. If still bleeding, go to hospital.
dontDo: Do NOT tilt the head back — it sends blood into the throat.
Do NOT pack cotton wool deep into the nose.
Do NOT blow the nose for several hours after.

drowning-rescue.title: Drowning rescue
steps: Do NOT enter the water unless you are a trained swimmer with a flotation device.
Throw a rope, branch, bottle, or anything that floats. Pull them out by the rope.
Once on land, lay them flat on their back. Open the airway gently.
Check breathing for 10 seconds. If not breathing, give 5 rescue breaths first — drowning needs breaths before compressions.
Then begin 30 compressions and 2 breaths. Continue until ambulance arrives or they breathe on their own.
Even if they wake up, take them to hospital — secondary drowning can happen hours later.
dontDo: Do NOT enter the water without flotation.
Do NOT push on the belly to drain water — it causes vomiting.
Do NOT skip the hospital even if they seem fine.

snake-bite.title: Snake bite
steps: Move the victim AWAY from the snake. Keep them calm and still. Movement spreads venom.
Remove rings, watches, and tight clothes from the bitten limb before it swells.
Keep the bitten part BELOW the level of the heart. Support it. Do not lift.
With a pen, draw a circle around the swelling. Write the time. Note any new swelling line every 15 min.
Call 108 immediately. Say 'snake bite' so they bring antivenom.
If you can do it SAFELY from a distance, photograph the snake. Do not chase it.
dontDo: Do NOT cut the wound or try to suck out venom.
Do NOT apply a tourniquet or tight band.
Do NOT give alcohol, pain killers, or food. Do NOT apply ice.

dog-bite.title: Dog bite (rabies risk)
steps: Get away from the dog. Note the dog's appearance and behaviour.
Wash the bite with soap and running clean water for at least 15 minutes — this washes out the virus.
Apply an antiseptic (povidone iodine / Dettol) if available. Pat dry.
Cover loosely with a clean cloth — do not seal tightly. Control bleeding if any with light pressure.
GO to a hospital today for anti-rabies vaccine and tetanus shot. Rabies is 100% fatal once symptoms start.
If the dog was wild or behaving oddly, mention this so the doctor gives immune globulin too.
dontDo: Do NOT apply turmeric, oil, chilli, or home remedies — they trap the virus in.
Do NOT stitch the wound at home.
Do NOT skip the rabies vaccine — it works within hours.

scorpion-sting.title: Scorpion or bee sting
steps: Keep the victim calm. Move them away from the swarm.
For a bee sting, SCRAPE the stinger out with a card edge. Do not pinch it with fingers — that pumps more venom in.
Wash the area with soap and water. Apply a cool cloth wrapped over ice to reduce pain and swelling.
Remove rings or tight bands near the sting before swelling.
Watch for severe allergy: face swelling, breathing trouble, dizziness. Treat as anaphylaxis.
For Indian red scorpion or for a stung child, go to hospital — antivenom can be life saving.
dontDo: Do NOT cut the sting or try to suck out venom.
Do NOT apply ice directly to the skin.
Do NOT ignore symptoms in a child.

poisoning-ingestion.title: Poisoning (swallowed)
steps: Find and KEEP the container, packet, or plant. Bring it to hospital with the patient.
Call 108 immediately. Say what was swallowed and how much, if known.
If they are awake, rinse the mouth with water. Have them spit it out.
Lay an unconscious victim on their side — recovery position — to protect the airway.
If they stop breathing, start CPR. Use a face shield if available.
Note the time of swallowing. Note any vomiting or change in behaviour for the doctor.
dontDo: Do NOT make them vomit unless poison control tells you to.
Do NOT give milk, water, or 'antidotes' without medical advice.
Do NOT wait to see what happens.

chemical-burn.title: Chemical burn
steps: Wear gloves if available, or use cloth between your hand and the chemical.
If the chemical is a dry powder, brush it off the skin first before adding water.
Flush the affected area with running cool water for at least 20 minutes — keep water flowing.
While flushing, remove contaminated clothes and jewellery near the burn.
Cover loosely with a clean non-stick cloth or cling film. Identify the chemical for the hospital.
Treat for shock: lay flat, keep warm, raise legs unless burned. Call 108.
dontDo: Do NOT rub the area — it spreads the chemical.
Do NOT apply a neutraliser (acid + base produces heat).
Do NOT pull off cloth stuck to the burn.

acid-attack.title: Acid attack on face / eyes
steps: Call 108 right away. Say 'acid attack — face and eyes'.
Flush the face and eyes with running cool clean water for at least 30 minutes — never stop the flow early.
Tell them to keep blinking under the water. Hold the eyelid open if they cannot.
Carefully remove contaminated clothes — cut them off rather than pulling over the head.
Cover the burned skin with a clean WET cloth — keep it cool and moist.
Note what was thrown — photo of the container if available — for the hospital and police.
dontDo: Do NOT rub the eyes or skin.
Do NOT apply oil, ghee, turmeric, or any home remedy.
Do NOT use any 'neutraliser' — only large amounts of clean water.

emergency-childbirth.title: Emergency childbirth
steps: Call 108 immediately. Say 'labour, baby coming'. Note time of regular contractions.
Lay the mother on her back with knees bent and legs apart. Support her head and shoulders.
Wash your hands. Place a clean cloth or sari under her hips.
When the baby's head appears, support it with one hand. DO NOT pull. Let the baby rotate naturally.
Once the baby is fully out, gently wipe the mouth and nose. Lay the baby on the mother's chest, skin to skin.
Cover both with a clean dry cloth. Keep them warm. Do NOT cut the cord without sterile tools — wait for help.
dontDo: Do NOT pull on the baby or the cord.
Do NOT slap or shake the baby.
Do NOT panic — most births complete safely without help.
